Significance: Founded by Bill Toro, privately owned corporation, known for appearing in movies and TV shows, unique delivery system using various vehicles, partnerships with Kozmo.com, Ford Motor Company, and Postmates
Historical Context: Founded in 1987, appeared in movies and TV shows, delivery system using different vehicles, partnerships with Kozmo.com, Ford Motor Company, and Postmates

Significance: LGBTQ+ pride event and parade in Canterbury, Kent since 2016, rescheduled in 2020 due to COVID-19, exhibition at Beaney House of Art and Knowledge, significant growth in attendance, featured various artists and performers
Historical Context: First event in 2016, exhibition in 2020, notable attendees and performers, historical growth and impact on the community

Significance: Three-day annual gay pride event, first pride event in Central PA in late 1980s, held at various locations before settling in Riverfront Park in 1992, features musical acts and drag queens, cancelled in 2020 and 2021 due to COVID-19, anticipated return in 2022
Historical Context: First Pride to take place on a US State Capitol in 2018, includes Voice United pre-Pride concert, Pride Festival with entertainment, Family Zone, vendors, and food, Spirits United Sunday church services, stub article on LGBT topics and Harrisburg, Pennsylvania-related article

Significance: Annual LGBT Pride celebration in Hull, organised by Hull LGBT+ Community Pride, first event in 2001, UK Pride in 2017, part of UK City of Culture celebrations
Historical Context: Inaugural event in Queen's Gardens, moved to West Park in 2011, relaunched as Pride in Hull in 2015, designated UK Pride in 2017, significant growth in attendance and performers

Significance: First pride parade in South Africa, held in Johannesburg, marked the beginning of pride events in the country, platform for political advocacy against discrimination and hate crimes targeting the LGBT community
Founders: Gay and Lesbian Organisation of the Witwatersrand, GLOW
Historical Context: Initiated and organized by GLOW, attended by 800 people, addressed by Simon Nkoli, Donné Rundle, Beverly Ditsie, Edwin Cameron, and Hendrik Pretorius, first event on the African continent

Significance: Promotes media arts for LGBT community, screenings at various venues, co-founded by Loris Bradley, Sarah Gish, and Marian Luntz
Founders: Loris Bradley, Sarah Gish, Marian Luntz
Historical Context: First held in 1997, renamed in 2007, no curator, venues book films independently, advisory board organizes films and events

Event Name: Queens Pride Parade and Multicultural Festival
Significance: Second oldest and second largest pride parade in NYC, founded by Daniel Dromm and Maritza Martinez, memorializes Julio Rivera, raises LGBTQ visibility in Queens
Location: Jackson Heights, Queens, New York City
Historical Context: Founded after the murder of Julio Rivera in 1990, serves as a response to hate crimes and rejection of multicultural curriculum in Queens

Significance: Non-corporate, community-funded LGBTQIA Pride event, organized by activists Daniel Villarreal and Chaaz Quigley, banned from Dallas Pride events in 2020
Historical Context: First held in 2014, inspired by QueerBomb in Austin, Texas, aims for a pride celebration without corporate influence, controversial article led to ban in 2020

Significance: Longest continuously run parade for LGBT people in Japan, held annually, last held in 2013
Historical Context: Established in 1996, aimed to support LGBT individuals, last held in 2013, considered a significant event in Japan for the LGBT community

Significance: International film festival for LGBT audiences, held in Tokyo, formerly known as TILGFF, established in 1992, held at various venues, includes Rainbow Reel Competition
Historical Context: Established in 1992, venue changes over the years, Rainbow Reel Competition started in 1995, awards for best film and director

Significance: Celebrates LGBTQIA+ community and allies, canceled in 2020 due to COVID-19, aims for equal rights and treatment, held in popular gay holiday destination, organized by local LGBTQI+ rights organizations
Founders: Ra.Ne., ACQUE, Arcigay Salento, LA COLLETTIVA, LeA
Historical Context: Inception in 2015, typically held in Gallipoli, moved to Brindisi in 2020, part of Puglia's gay-friendly environment, part of three annual Pride parades in Puglia

Significance: Celebrates LGBT people and allies, largest gathering of LGBT people in the nation, world-renowned parade
Historical Context: 49th annual parade in 2019 with 289 contingents, parade route along Market Street, hundreds of thousands of spectators, two-day festival around the parade

Significance: World's oldest transgender film festival, originally named Tranny Fest, co-founded by Christopher Lee and Alex Austin, Elise Hurwitz as technical director
Founders: Christopher Lee, Alex Austin, Elise Hurwitz
Historical Context: Originally organized by volunteers without government or private funding, held biennially until 2005, transformed into an annual event due to audience demand, held in various venues before settling at the Roxie Theater in 2013

Significance: Sexuality rights festival featuring talks, forums, workshops, art, music, and film screenings, loosely translated as "Sexuality Independence," banned in 2011
Historical Context: Founded in 2008 coinciding with Merdeka celebrations, theme of "Our Bodies, Our Rights" in 2009, banned in 2011 due to potential public disorder

Significance: First and largest LGBT event in mainland China, held from 2009 to 2020, featured cultural events, art exhibitions, film screenings, and parties
Founders: Tiffany Lemay, Hannah Miller, Shanghai LGBT community
Historical Context: Organized by volunteers, initially framed as a private festival without a public parade to avoid official attention, celebrated 10th year in 2018, ended in 2020

Significance: Annual LGBT film festival in Shanghai, founded in 2017, volunteer-run, focuses on Asian film, promotes diversity and dialogue
Historical Context: First held in September 2017, aims to showcase queer Asian characters, encourage discussion on queer culture in the East, represents diversity in love and freedom

Significance: Established to promote tolerance for the LGBT community through film, part of ShanghaiPRIDE, free screenings, workshops, and discussions
Historical Context: Launched in 2015, supports new queer Chinese filmmaking talent, part of ShanghaiPRIDE, features workshops and panel discussions

Significance: Peaceful march of LGBT individuals and allies, combines social and political protest with entertainment, held since 2008, inspired by Stonewall riots, significant human rights demonstration in Bulgaria
Historical Context: First parade in 2008, held on same date as Stonewall riots, legalizing same-sex activity in 1968, post-communism gay venues emerged, polarizing event, supported by national and international partners, organized by volunteers and NGOs

Event Name: The Way OUT West Film Fest
Significance: Showcases gay and lesbian films from around the world, includes Q&A sessions, after-parties, and various events, produced by Closet Cinema, aims to highlight the LGBT experience
Location: Albuquerque, New Mexico and Santa Fe, New Mexico
Annual Occurrence: Annually in late September to early October
Historical Context: Founded in 2003, expanded to Santa Fe, concurrent screenings in Albuquerque and Santa Fe, films from various genres and origins, selection process through private screenings and film festivals
trigger: Southwest Gay and Lesbian Film Festival

Significance: Second LGBT pride event in Croatia after Zagreb, organized by NGOs Kontra, Iskorak, and feminist organization 'Dominoes', faced violent opposition from anti-gay protesters, led to cancellation of the event, sparked support for LGBT community
Historical Context: First event in 2011, concerns about public reaction in conservative city, 300 participants, police accused of inadequate protection, violent riot with 10,000 anti-gay protesters, activists led to safety, support campaign led to Zagreb Pride participation, meeting with President Ivo Josipović, 10th Zagreb Pride march with 4,000 participants, largest Pride rally in Croatia at the time, peaceful event with police protection

Significance: South West Wales LGBT festival, first event in 2009, organised by local LGBT individuals, raised money for Terrence Higgins Trust, became a registered charity in 2010
Historical Context: First event in 2009, successful fundraising for charity, transition to registered charity in 2010, name change to Spectrum South West Wales in 2013, cancellation in 2015 due to financial difficulties, revival in 2016 with new volunteers

Significance: Pride festival with Sápmi focus, organized by Queering Sápmi, features performances, workshops, and parades in different Sápmi regions
Historical Context: Started in 2014 in Kiruna, Lapland, traveled to Karasjok, Inari, and Östersund in subsequent years, showcases Sápmi culture and LGBTQ+ visibility

Significance: Largest LGBTQ pride parade in East Asia, held in Taipei, attracts over 200,000 participants, organized by Taiwan LGBT Pride Community
Historical Context: First held in 2003, largest pride event in Asia, social movement focus, little corporate sponsorship, shares road with traffic and bystanders

Significance: International film award for LGBT-themed films, presented by independent jury, part of Berlinale, founded by Wieland Speck and Manfred Salzgeber, named after teddy bear trophy
Annual Occurrence: Annually during Berlin International Film Festival
Historical Context: Founded in 1987, initially named Teddy Bear Award, awarded to feature, short, and documentary films, celebrated 30th anniversary with "Teddy30" lineup in 2016

Significance: Largest LGBTQ Parade in Asia, attracts over 250,000 attendees, marks progress of gay rights in Israel from criminalization to decriminalization in 1988, first Pride event in 1993
Historical Context: First Pride Parade in 1993, attracts 200,000+ participants, significant event in LGBTQ rights movement in Israel

Significance: One-week celebration leading up to Transgender Day of Remembrance (TDoR) on November 20, educates about transgender and gender non-conforming individuals, hosts educational events, first week of November typically features third-party events, second week focuses on main central venue
Historical Context: Educational events include movie screenings (e.g., Paris is Burning), personal testimonies, discussions on trans-themed books, art shows, and performances, expanded to month-long celebration in San Francisco in 2018, commendations by San Francisco Board of Supervisors in 2019

Significance: Memorializes those murdered due to transphobia, founded to draw attention to violence against transgender individuals, Gwendolyn Ann Smith among founders
Historical Context: Founded in response to Rita Hester's murder, observed in over 185 cities in 2010, includes reading of names of victims, various memorial activities

Significance: Nonprofit organization hosting LGBT celebration, features pride parade, festival, and block party, draws nearly 400,000 attendees, named after Ashley Rukes, began as 50-person protest march in 1972
Historical Context: Inception as protest march in 1972, expansion of events in 1990s, name changes to include bisexual and transgender, addition of music stages in mid-1990s, diverse activities in mid-2010s

Significance: Europe's largest celebration for African, Asian, Middle Eastern, Latin American, and Caribbean heritage LGBTQ+ individuals, founded by Phyllis Akua Opoku-Gyimah, attracts nearly 8,000 annually
Historical Context: Started in 2005 as a day trip to Southend-on-Sea, approximately 7,500 attendees in 2018, partnered with Stonewall in 2019, featured in British Vogue interview in 2019

Significance: Celebrates Utah's diversity and LGBTQ+ community, includes parade, film festival, Dyke March, interfaith service, charity run, and related events
Founders: Utah Pride Center, Salt Lake Coalition for Human Rights, Affirmation: LGBTQ Mormons, Families, & Friends, Utah Pride Inc.
Historical Context: Started in 1977, evolved from a three-day conference, various activities and speakers, significant LGBTQ+ organizations involved

Significance: Pride parade in Varese, Italy, one of three in Lombardy, member of InterPride and EPOA, first event had 3,000 participants
Historical Context: First event in 2016, patronage from various organizations except Municipality of Varese, slogan "Esistiamo" meaning Resist and exist

Significance: Austrian pride parade, Rainbow Parade on Vienna Ring Road, first event in 1982, organised by HOSI Wien, established as regular event in 1996, managed by CSD Wien until 2001, currently organised by HOSI Wien
Founders: HOSI Wien, Austrian Lesbian and Gay Forum, CSD Wien, HOSI Vienna
Historical Context: First gay rights festival in 1982, first Pride parade in 1984, Europride host in 2001 and 2019, Vienna Pride Village, Vienna Pride Run started in 2018

Significance: LGBT pride parade and demonstration in Ankara, Turkey, held annually to mark the end of Ankara pride week, participants gather in Cebeci, important public space in Ankara
Historical Context: First event in 2003, occurs on last Sunday of June or first Sunday of July, aims to raise awareness about LGBT rights, part of Ankara's cultural events

Significance: Sporting and cultural event open to all, licensed by the Gay and Lesbian International Sport Association, separate from the Gay Games, 18,599 participants in 2006
Founders: Gay and Lesbian International Sport Association, Montreal, Quebec, Canada, various sponsors
Historical Context: Developed as a response to the 2006 Gay Games controversy, held in Montreal with significant international participation

Significance: Promotes LGBTQ issues internationally, organized by InterPride, host cities selected annually, largest LGBTQ Pride event for the year
Historical Context: Inaugural event in Rome faced controversy due to opposition from the Vatican and conservative politicians, significant impact on LGBTQ visibility and advocacy efforts

Significance: First successful pride march in Southeast Europe, inspired by Stonewall Riots and Gay Liberation Front, self-identified as LGBTIQ+ march, name changed in 2003, organized by volunteer-based committee, supported by various organizations, member of InterPride, EPOA, IGLYO, ILGA-Europe, founding member of Center for LGBT Equality, funded by City of Zagreb and international organizations
Founders: Volunteer-based committee, Multimedia Institute, Iskorak, Kontra, CMS, InterPride, EPOA, IGLYO, ILGA-Europe, Center for LGBT Equality
Historical Context: First march in 2002, name change in 2003, logistical support from various organizations, establishment of NGO in 2008, member of multiple LGBT organizations, founding member of national LGBT association, funding sources, annual march and Pride Week events since 2011
